finding: §6.1 lists the close paths that record commit_files as 'tp commit, tp done --auto-commit, tp done --commit' but omits 'tp done --batch', which is tp's PRIMARY close mechanism (CLAUDE.md, done.go runDoneBatch records commit_shas at L783-785). Every batch-closed task would carry commit_shas but no commit_files, so §5 prior-work file lists and §10.2's 'file changed since' signal would be silently empty for the most common close path. This breaks Foundational Principle P2 (Batch Parity): what is recorded for one close is recorded for N.
suggestion: Add 'tp done --batch (rows carrying commit_shas)' to the §6.1 list and add an acceptance case mirroring test #6 for a batch close.

finding: The close recipe places the commit flags AFTER the `--` separator: builtin `tp done <id> -- "<evidence>" --auto-commit` and hc `tp done <id> -- "<evidence>" --commit <sha>`. In cobra/pflag `--` terminates flag parsing, so `--auto-commit`/`--commit` are swallowed as positional args; resolveMultiReason (done.go:885, 'last arg is reason, rest are task IDs') then treats the flag token as the reason and the evidence as a second task ID. A fresh unit copying the recipe verbatim fails to close (wrong reason, task-not-found, or — under hc — 'needs --commit' because the flag was never parsed). The working form, per done.go's own Example and hcCommitHint (commitstrategy.go:13), puts flags BEFORE `--`: `tp done <id> --auto-commit -- "<evidence>"` and `tp done <id> --commit <sha> -- "<evidence>"`. §8.4 mandates `--` before the reason, so the fix is purely flag ordering. This defeats §8's stated purpose ('the exact commands') and is the first thing a unit runs.

Contradiction between the resume brief_command and the unit recipe. §9.3 sets the implement-phase brief_command to `tp brief <id>`, which §9.1 defines as read-only ('claims nothing'). But §11.3 and §13.3 prescribe `tp next --brief` as the unit's first call precisely because it claims (sets started_at), which is why §11.3 calls `claimed` the normal case for brief-driven units. An orchestrator that follows resume's brief_command (`tp brief <id>`) never claims, so the later `tp done` performs the implicit open->wip claim and records duration_source=implicit — directly contradicting §11.3 and defeating the duration-provenance goal of §11. The spec must state which command the implement unit runs; if resume is meant to hand the orchestrator a preview, brief_command should be `tp next --brief` (or §11.3 must drop the 'already claimed' claim).

The prior-round audit section promises, per non-PASS row, 'whether a commit touching that file landed after the round was recorded'. 'That file' is undefined when the evidence location is not a file path (e.g. a spec anchor like '§8.1', a line range, or a multi-file finding), and no field name is given for the boolean. A tester cannot assert on a specific JSON key, nor predict the result when location↔file is not 1:1. Specify the field name and the location→file resolution (or state that non-file locations report 'unknown').

The prior-round section lists, per non-PASS row, 'whether a commit touching that file landed after the round was recorded'. This presupposes every non-PASS row has a file, but audit rows for spec-derived findings (table_row / list_item / task_acceptance) and findings whose evidence_file is null have no file. The behavior for file-less findings is unspecified (omit the field? report 'n/a'? exclude the row?). An implementer must invent it, and the choice changes what the auditor sees as 'possibly fixed since last round'.

§10.2's audit prior-round section must carry 'whether a commit touching that file landed after the round was recorded'. Audit rounds are not tasks, so §6's per-task commit_files does not cover this; the spec never names the data source (git log since recorded_at? done-task commit_shas? both?), nor how amended/rebased commits are handled (committer time vs author time vs the recorded_at wall clock). This is load-bearing for the 'did this file change since the last round' signal the whole section exists to provide.
suggestion: Specify the exact query (e.g. 'git log --name-only --since=<recorded_at> -- <file>') and the timestamp basis, and note the amended-commit caveat.

finding: The new file-derived id `file-<role>-<slug>` leaves the slug function undefined: no lowercasing rule, no handling of path separators/extension, no length cap, and no collision policy when two items share the same subject. Since cross-round stability is the entire point of the change, the exact derivation is load-bearing and an implementer inventing it risks breaking the (role, item_id) dedup the fix exists to repair. Additionally `<role>` is ambiguous: the current idPrefix is an abbreviation ('file-sec-'/'file-maint-', audit_roles.go:54-55) while the spec's example 'file-go-safety-2' implies a full role id — state whether it is the abbreviation, the built-in id, or the custom role file stem.

'inlines file contents for the role whose checklist exhausts the reading budget first' references an undefined quantity — 'the reading budget' is never given a value, a config key, or a unit (bytes? tokens? lines?), so a tester cannot predict when the budget is exhausted. Worse, 'the role ... first' is unspecified: first by roles-array order, by alphabetical role id, by checklist size? Two implementers inline content for different roles, and Test #14 ('states whether contents are complete or must be read') cannot be set up deterministically without knowing which role is inlined.

Test #18 requires constructing a fixture of 'a round recorded before this release, whose item ids are positional' and asserting tp reads it 'without error' and flags the ids as legacy. But the spec never states the detection heuristic — how does tp decide a recorded round is legacy? By the id matching `file-<role>-<numeric>`? By a schema version stamp? A numeric slug collides with the positional shape, so the rule is non-obvious. Without the heuristic a tester cannot build a fixture that reliably triggers the legacy branch versus the current `file-<role>-<slug>` branch.

§10.9 requires a prior-round section built from a pre-release round to 'state that its ids are legacy and not comparable', but ReviewRound (reviewstate.go:17-28) carries no schema or binary-version marker, and a stored item_id like 'file-sec-0' is indistinguishable from a new slug that happens to collide. v0.25.0 solved the identical problem for roles_hash by defining 'empty == pre-v0.25.0 == treat as matching'; this spec proposes no analogous marker, so tp has no deterministic way to tell a legacy round from a current one.
suggestion: Add a marker on ReviewRound (an id_scheme field, or reuse recorded_at against a recorded upgrade time) and define legacy detection as explicitly as the roles_hash empty-string rule.

finding: duration_source is a stored field that drives estimation_accuracy exclusion, yet the spec never adds it to the managed set (set.go:19 lists status/started_at/closed_at/closed_reason/gate_passed_at/commit_sha/commit_shas/gate_skipped_reason). A derived provenance field that affects a report metric must be tp-owned or it is gameable. The spec also leaves its value undefined for two paths the close code already supports: (a) 'tp done --batch' rows carrying an explicit started_at (done.go:729-733) — claimed or implicit?; (b) a task claimed via tp next, reopened (clearing started_at), then closed by a bare tp done (implicit claim) — does the earlier claim's provenance survive?
suggestion: Add duration_source to the managed set, and specify its value for batch explicit-started_at rows and for the claim→reopen→done sequence.

finding: report.go today excludes tasks whose actual_minutes rounds to 0.0 (excluded_from_accuracy, L129-132). An implicit-duration task produces a near-zero duration that ALSO rounds to 0.0, so it satisfies BOTH exclusion rules. §11.2 says the implicit count is 'reported separately from tasks excluded for rounding to zero', implying the buckets are disjoint, but never states the precedence (duration_source checked first, or rounding first?) nor names the new summary field. Without precedence the two counts overlap and the summary is ambiguous.
suggestion: Specify the evaluation order (e.g., duration_source==implicit excludes first, independently of rounding) and name the new summary field (e.g. excluded_implicit) alongside the existing excluded_from_accuracy.

finding: The sixth brief section 'What follows — the tasks this unit unblocks, and the phase this unit's completion moves toward' feeds no decision a fresh implementation unit is permitted to make. §4.2/§3.3 mandate one-unit-then-stop; §4.3 makes the verbatim acceptance the sole success condition; §7.1 forbids implementing anything beyond that acceptance ('implement only what this task's acceptance criteria require'). So the unit can neither pick the next task, decide the phase, nor optimize its code for downstream tasks (the fence explicitly bars looking past acceptance). 'What follows' therefore has no permitted use under the spec's own fence — it is pure inbound token cost on every implementation brief, and at worst a temptation that contradicts §7.1. This is the one section that fails P6 ('every added byte replaces a byte the orchestrator was typing'): the orchestrator, not the unit, consumes unblock/phase info, and the orchestrator already gets it from `tp resume`/`tp plan`.
suggestion: Drop 'What follows' from the unit-facing brief (and the `next` key from the §4.4 JSON). Phase/unblock context belongs to the orchestrator's existing surfaces (`tp resume` next_action, `tp plan`), not to a unit that is forbidden to act on it. If retained, narrow it to a single line ('completing this moves the project toward the <phase> phase') and justify why a fenced unit may read it.

finding: `--json` returns BOTH the assembled `brief` text AND its decomposed parts `{task, prior_work, close, scope, next}`. The parts ARE the brief broken into pieces, so every non-compact JSON call emits the unit's content twice (once assembled, once decomposed) — near-doubling the payload on tp's primary machine-readable path. The stated justification (§4.4 'mirroring how `tp review` returns both prompts[].prompt and structured fields') is a false equivalence: in `tp review` the structured fields (Round, RequiredCleanRounds, ConsecutiveClean, convergence) are ORTHOGONAL metadata about the round, not a decomposition of the prompt text; here `{task,prior_work,close,scope,next}` are literally the brief re-emitted piecewise. A consumer either pastes `brief` into a subagent OR assembles from the parts — never both. §12.2 confirms the parts exist for 'a driver that assembles its own prompt', in which case `brief` is dead weight in that same JSON, and vice-versa.
suggestion: Split the contract by flag, not duplicate: `tp brief --json` returns `{brief}` (assembled text, default); `tp brief --json --parts` (or `--decompose`) returns `{task,prior_work,close,scope,next}` for drivers that assemble their own. Dropping the always-on dual emission removes the doubling without losing either consumer.

finding: `--json` returns `{brief, task, prior_work, close, scope, next}` described as 'its machine-readable parts', but: (a) the mapping from these 5 fields to the 6 sections in §4.1 is never stated (a reader must infer task↔'Your unit', close↔'How to close', next↔'What follows'); and (b) the Identity section has no corresponding structured field — it exists only inside `brief`. A driver consuming `scope`/`close` structurally has no way to read Identity the same way. Either add an `identity` field or state that Identity is intentionally text-only, and document the field↔section mapping.

§5.4 makes dependency-derived entries undroppable and §6.4 caps each task's file list at 50, but no section caps the TOTAL prior-work size. A task with a wide transitive fan-in (e.g. 20 done deps) yields 20 × (evidence line + shas + up to 50 paths) in a single non-compact brief — potentially 1000 file paths — which violates Principle P3 / §2.6 ('every added byte replaces a byte the orchestrator was typing') for exactly the context the brief is meant to bound. --compact (§12.1) drops file lists, but the default brief has no ceiling.
suggestion: Cap total prior-work bytes or entry count (deps first, recency second), and state the cap plus an 'N more omitted' note mirroring §5.4's recency disclosure.

finding: '--prior <n> overrides the recency count (default 5, range 0–20)' pins the valid range but never states the out-of-range behavior. A tester cannot assert whether `--prior 25` or `--prior -1` exits 2 (usage), clamps silently, or errors with code 1/3. The only test (#4) exercises `--prior 0`; the negative boundary (the other end of the stated range, and beyond it) is untested and unspecified.

'tp resolves each sha's changed paths' never specifies which git change classes count as a path: added, modified, deleted, and renamed-old vs renamed-new are all distinct in git. The section frames the array as 'what a closed unit produced', yet a deleted file is not produced — one implementer includes deletes, another excludes them; rename sides are equally ambiguous. Test #6 ('records commit_files') cannot be written deterministically without a defined change-type set. Pin the set (e.g. A+M+R-target, excluding D) explicitly.

§6.2 declares commit_files a managed field (tp set rejects it, tp reopen clears it), but §6.4 introduces its companion commit_files_total and never adds it to the managed set. An agent could run 'tp set <id> commit_files_total=3' to falsify the 'list is partial' signal, or leave a stale total after reopen clears commit_files (the existing reopen in reopen.go:54-59 clears StartedAt/CommitSHAs but the spec only names commit_files for the new clear). A count field that qualifies a capped array must be protected and cleared jointly with the array.
suggestion: State explicitly that commit_files AND commit_files_total are both managed and both cleared by tp reopen.

finding: 'a deduplicated, sorted array' (§6.1) and 'stores the first 50 in sorted order' (§6.4) never define the comparator. Repo-root-relative paths could be sorted by raw byte order, case-insensitively, or directory-then-file; the 'first 50' chosen when the cap is hit depends entirely on that choice. Test #7 asserts '50 sorted paths' but with no defined order the assertion is circular — two engineers pick different first-50 sets.

§6.4 caps commit_files at 50 paths while §7.3 routes the field into 'the audit phase's file selection'. The spec never states whether audit now reads the stored commit_files or keeps deriving uncapped from commit_shas (existing --affected-from-tasks, audit.go:730). If audit consumes commit_files it inherits two silent gaps: (a) the 50-cap truncates selection for a task touching >50 files (commit_files_total records the count but the selection still misses files), and (b) §6.3 omits the field entirely when git was unavailable at close, so a task closed then would contribute NO files to audit even though its commit_shas could resolve later. State audit's source explicitly and require it not to inherit the cap/omission.

The close-recipe table lists only `builtin` and `hc`, but `auto` is a documented valid `commit_strategy` value (per the project conventions: `commit_strategy=<builtin|auto|hc>`). §8.1 says tp 'resolves the effective commit_strategy and emits the matching recipe', yet gives no recipe for `auto` and no rule for how `auto` resolves to builtin/hc. A tester cannot write a pass/fail assertion for what recipe a brief shows when the effective strategy is `auto`; two implementers could emit the builtin recipe, the hc recipe, or an auto-specific one. Test #8 only covers `builtin` and `hc`.

§8.3 states the evidence format as 'a contract' with 'no bare done/wip/deferred', but no acceptance test in §15 pins rejection of a bare-word reason and §9 lists no change to tp done's validation. If the rule is enforced, existing closes with bare-word reasons break (a behavior change hidden outside §9); if it is advisory (the brief tells the unit, tp done still accepts any string), calling it a 'contract' overstates it. Either reading is defensible but the spec must declare which.
suggestion: State explicitly whether VerifyClosure gains a bare-word rejection (and add a test), or downgrade 'contract' to 'guidance the brief communicates'.

finding: `tp brief [id]` accepts an explicit id, but the spec only defines exit 4 for 'no task available' (the no-argument path) and implies success otherwise. It never states what happens for an id that does not exist or is not ready (already-done, blocked, unknown id). A tester cannot pin the exit code (2 usage? 3 file? 4 state?) or the emitted message shape for `tp brief <bad-id>`, which is the first failure mode a real caller hits.

'A file-derived checklist item's id becomes file-<role>-<slug>' leaves two gaps: (1) the slug derivation is unspecified — lowercase? spaces→dashes? truncation length? hash tail for collisions? — so a test can assert stability (same subject→same id) but cannot assert a concrete id string; and (2) the qualifier 'file-derived' implies non-file-derived items exist, whose id scheme is entirely unstated. Pin the slug algorithm and cover the non-file case (or state all items are file-derived).

resolved.evidence: accepted: below convergence bar; spec comprehensive

finding: The stable audit item id becomes `file-<role>-<slug>` where slug is 'derived from the item's own subject (the file path and the checklist text)'. The spec sets no length cap on the slug. A checklist item's text is frequently a full clause ('the request handler must validate input before touching the database'), so an untruncated slug can be long, and that id is then carried in EVERY NDJSON result row, in the §10.2 prior-round section for every non-PASS row, and across ALL rounds — so slug inflation compounds per round and per role. Stable ids are earned (they fix §10.3 dedup), but an unbounded slug turns the dedup key itself into a recurring token cost.

resolved.evidence: accepted: below convergence bar; spec comprehensive

suggestion: Cap the slug (e.g. first 3–4 dash-segments, or N chars) and state the truncation rule in §10.3. Stability only requires the slug to be a deterministic function of the subject, not a faithful copy of it.

finding: §10.4 fixes output_path as 'relative to the working directory', but round and snapshot files live in .tp-review/<spec-base>/ (reviewstate.go:71-75, frequently a subdirectory), and tp audit is commonly invoked as 'tp audit spec/0.30.0.md' from the repo root — so cwd, the spec directory, and the state directory diverge. The spec does not say whether the unit writes the file at cwd, beside the spec, or beside the state dir, which determines the path the agent must hand back to --record and the glob the merge step relies on.

resolved.evidence: accepted: below convergence bar; spec comprehensive

suggestion: Pin one anchor (cwd is fine) and state that --record receives that same path, so emission and merge agree.

finding: §10.6 requires EVERY emitted prompt body to name four quantities: round number, required clean-round count, consecutive clean rounds so far, and remaining budget. Of these, only the round number (already injected for review, review.go:765) genuinely alters a role's examination — it tells a reviewer/auditor 'focus on new/residual issues, this is a convergence pass'. The required-clean count and consecutive-clean-so-far are orchestrator convergence bookkeeping (the decision they feed — 'are we converged?' — is made by `tp review/audit --status`, not by the role), so injecting them into 3–4 role prompts per round adds tokens each role cannot act on. The clean-round numerics already live in the JSON metadata (review.go:46-47 RequiredCleanRounds/ConsecutiveClean); restating them in every prompt body is body-vs-metadata duplication.

resolved.evidence: accepted: below convergence bar; spec comprehensive

suggestion: Keep round number + a one-clause framing ('first pass' vs 'convergence check, focus on residual/regressed issues') in the prompt body; leave the required/consecutive/budget numerics in the structured JSON metadata only. An orchestrator that needs the counts already reads them from the JSON, not from re-parsing prompt text.

finding: `duration_source` is a new per-task stored field whose only named consumer is the human-facing `tp report` (exclusion from estimation_accuracy, separate count). No agent decision reads it: it is not in the brief, not in `tp next`/`tp show` acceptance, and does not change how a unit implements or closes. Under P6 it therefore replaces no byte the orchestrator was typing — it is net-new stored + emitted bytes for reporting quality. The motivating bug (§11.1, 17 tasks silently excluded) is real, but the fix is a report-accuracy field, not an AX field.

resolved.evidence: accepted: below convergence bar; spec comprehensive

suggestion: Confine `duration_source` strictly to `tp report` output and keep it out of `tp show`/`tp next`/brief JSON (the spec currently only commits `tp report` to carrying it — make that exclusivity explicit so it never leaks into agent-consumed surfaces). Accept the small report-only cost; do not let it spread.

finding: 'the identity and scope sections shorten to a single line each' does not say what is on those single lines. Test #17 verifies presence/absence (drops file lists + excerpt, keeps acceptance + close recipe + scope prohibitions) but the 'single line each' claim itself is untestable — a tester cannot assert the compact identity/scope content because it is undefined. Specify the single-line form (e.g. identity = 'Implement <id>; one unit, then stop.') or drop the 'single line' claim and keep only the keep/drop list.

resolved.evidence: accepted: below convergence bar; spec comprehensive

finding: The 'What follows' section must state 'the phase this unit's completion moves toward', but the existing phase detector (AssembleResume / BuildNextAction) reports the CURRENT phase, not a forward projection. §14.5 forbids any change to phase detection, yet the brief requires a projection the current detector does not produce, and the spec never defines how it is computed when multiple tasks remain (e.g. 'still implement' vs 'last task → audit').

resolved.evidence: accepted: below convergence bar; spec comprehensive

suggestion: Define the projection rule (e.g. 'last remaining open task → the next phase per BuildNextAction; otherwise current phase') or narrow 'What follows' to the unblocked-task list only.

finding: The `--json` shape `{brief, task, prior_work, close, scope, next}` gives structured fields for 5 of the 6 brief sections but none for Identity. A driver that assembles its own prompt from the JSON parts alone drops the reset-discipline text ('one unit then stop'). The spec doesn't state why identity is text-only; if intentional, say so, otherwise add an `identity` field for parity.

resolved.evidence: accepted: below convergence bar; spec comprehensive

finding: `--prior <n>` (default 5, range 0–20) is a fine-grained selection knob whose stated pressure — keeping the brief small — is already absorbed by `--compact` (§12.1 collapses each prior entry to one line and drops file lists). An agent under token budget reaches for `--compact`, not for guessing whether 3 or 5 prior entries fit. Only `--prior 0` (drop recency, keep dependency-derived entries) is a genuinely distinct selection from `--compact`; the rest of the 0–20 range is surface an agent is unlikely to exercise. A knob that almost never gets turned is spec/flag/documentation/README overhead (§13.2) without removing a round-trip.

resolved.evidence: accepted: below convergence bar; spec comprehensive

suggestion: Ship only the distinct case: keep dependency-derived entries always, and expose recency as a boolean `--no-recent-prior` (or fold recency control into `--compact`). Drop the numeric range unless dogfooding shows an agent actually tuning the count.

finding: §6.1 frames 'a close that records commits (tp commit, tp done --auto-commit, tp done --commit)', but `tp commit` records a sha on a wip task WITHOUT closing (the v0.30 builtin recipe in §8.1 uses --auto-commit, not tp commit). It is unspecified whether commit_files is populated at the intermediate `tp commit` step or only at the subsequent tp done (iterating commit_shas). Acceptance #6 ('a close through each commit path') mostly disambiguates toward 'at close', but the §6.1 phrasing invites the wrong implementation.

resolved.evidence: accepted: below convergence bar; spec comprehensive

finding: 'one line per acceptance criterion' states the expected evidence shape, but the spec never says what happens on mismatch — fewer lines than criteria, more lines, or a missing trailing Out-of-scope marker. Test #9 only covers the well-formed trailing `Out of scope:` case. Without a defined behavior (accept-and-warn? reject with exit 1?) a tester cannot write a negative test for a malformed evidence block, which §8.3 frames as a contract.

resolved.evidence: accepted: below convergence bar; spec comprehensive

finding: `tp brief <id>` accepts an explicit id, but its behavior for a task that is already done, or whose dependencies are not yet done, is unspecified (no exit code, no message). Since §5.1 item 1 selects only done transitive deps, a brief for a blocked or done id would render an odd prior-work set. Define the error/exit (likely exit 4 with the {done,message} shape) for these targets.

resolved.evidence: accepted: below convergence bar; spec comprehensive
